0: Evaluation and life cycle assessment of a poultry litter anaerobic digester with nutrient capture
Amro Hassanein Andrew Moss b , Nicholas Cloyd b , Stephanie Lansing a,'
a Department of Environmental Science and Technology, University of Maryland, College Park, MD, USA b Planet Found Energy Development LLC, Pocomoke City, MD, USA

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Methane Biogas Nitrogen Phosphorus Climate change|This study evaluated a farm-scale (400 m³ anaerobic digestion (AD) system processing 11.2 tons/month of poultry litter (PL) with liquid recirculation and creation of three solid fertilizer products. An average CH4 pro- duction of 112 CH4/kg volatile solids (VS) was achieved during the 22-month monitoring period, which rose to 222 L CH4/kg VS (949 m³ CH4/month) over time. The average TKN and TP captured from solid separation were 36.1 % and 56.4%, respectively, with additional nutrients (12.9% TKN and 23.3 TP) captured from the filter press prior to liquid recirculation. The life cycle assessment (LCA) showed that if 3x more PL was processed in the AD system and the biogas was used in a combined heat and power (CHP) generator, the climate change impact would be 722 % lower and the eutrophication potential would be 510 % lower than the 'Baseline Sce- nario' of transporting unprocessed PL 40 km away for field application.
1: Anaerobic digestion (AD) is a series of microbial processes to break down biodegradable material in the absence of oxygen producing biogas composed of 50-70% methane (CH4), CO2 (30-50%), water vapor (3-4 %), and hydrogen sulfide (H2S: 500-8000 ppm, <0.8%) (Appels et al., 2008; Labatut and Pronto, 2018). AD is gaining traction as a viable technique for mitigating greenhouse gas emissions and converting low- value materials to higher-value materials, which include biogas and fertilizer (Beausang et al., 2020). Biogas can be produced from a variety of organic materials, such as poultry litter (PL), dairy manure, food waste, crop waste, or sewage sludge (Achi et al., 2020; Appels et al., 2008; Deena et al., 2022). PL is a less common AD substrate compared to other manure substrates, with <10 operational AD systems in the United States (US) utilizing PL (USEPA, 2022). However, previous studies have reported that PL had higher CH4 potential than dairy manure (Achi et al., 2020; Itodo and Awulu, 1999).

The PL substrate consists of animal waste, feed residue, feathers, and bedding material, including wood chips, and is cleaned out of poultry houses between flocks (Hassanein et al., 2021). According to Singh et al. (2010), the lignin-rich bedding material is not readily digested by anaerobic bacteria due to the fact that lignin is not easily biodegraded, and furthermore, the lignin binds to cellulose and hemicellulose,

decreasing the availability of polysaccharides for hydrolysis (Abdel- Hamid et al., 2013; Shrestha et al., 2017). Additionally, PL has a low moisture content and a high ammonia-nitrogen content within a com- plex organic matrix. Successful AD of PL often requires the addition of water to the relatively dry (~20% moisture content) manure prior to AD processing (Dadrasnia et al., 2021). The total solids (TS) content is a critical parameter in AD of PL, impacting hydrolysis and methano- genesis rates (Indren et al., 2020). Abbassi-Guendouz et al. (2012) showed that the daily CH4 yield from PL was reduced by 60% when the TS content increased from 10 to 30 % due to slower hydrolysis rates and slower diffusion of soluble intermediate products at the higher TS level (Xu et al., 2014). Additionally, increasing the TS concentration from 5 to 20 % resulted in a sevenfold drop in CH4 output in ammonia-stressed digesters (Li et al., 2013). To overcome the dry nature of PL, the liquid effluent can be used to wet the incoming PL, but this effluent is often enriched in ammonia (NH). In the literature, most previous research on AD of PL has focused on batch systems (Achi et al., 2020; Hassanein et al., 2019; Indren et al., 2020).

The PL substrate and AD effluent contain a wide range of soluble metals and nutrients, including high NH4, which can impact AD (Möller and Müller, 2012). There is a lack of comprehensive knowledge on nutrient capture technologies post-AD. There has not been previous work focused on an integrated, continuous AD system for digesting only

PL with nutrient capture and recycling the digester effluent to the front of the AD system at the farm-scale. This was the first large-scale, recir- culating AD system utilizing only PL with no liquid leaving the system.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) can be used to determine environmental impacts while comparing different scenarios, including product inputs, outputs, and potential environmental effects throughout the life cycle (ISO 14040, 2006). LCAs do not take into account market characteris- tics, such as price impacts, supply, and demand (Choudhury et al., 2020; Yang and Heijungs, 2018). A LCA is considered a suitable tool to eval- uate the environmental sustainability of AD systems (Czyrnek-Delêtre et al., 2017) and has been used extensively to evaluate AD of feedstocks,

such as cow manure, grass, maize, and food waste (Beausang et al., 2020; Tsapekos et al., 2019; Van Stappen et al., 2016). There are not any comprehensive LCA studies for farm-scale AD systems using only PL as the substrate with nutrient capture and effluent recirculation (Van Stappen et al., 2016). Most previous LCA research has focused on municipal solid waste, sewage sludge, dairy manure, or food waste (Arena et al., 2003; Astrup et al., 2009; Cherubini et al., 2009). Only one prior study has used LCA on a farm-scale AD system for PL in Poland, but the system operated without nutrient capture or effluent recirculation (Beausang et al., 2020).
This study is the first to investigate a larger-scale AD system (400 m³

Fig. 1. The overview of the sampling points of the 22-month study shown in (a), with the life cycle (LCA) assessment boundary, inputs, and outputs for the anaerobic digestion (AD) and nutrient capture system shown in (b).

using PL-only with a novel system to capture the nutrient and the solids from the digestate and recirculate the liquid from the filter press while evaluating the system using LCA. The aims of this study were: 1) eval- uate the energy, nutrient, and organic transformations for a novel farm- scale AD design that digested only PL over a 22-month monitoring period; 2) determine the effect of recirculating ammonia-rich, recycled water from the filter press to increase the moisture of PL substrate feeding the AD system; 3) quantify the environmental impacts of an AD system with nutrient capture to produce energy from PL and extract nutrients from the PL matrix using LCA; and 4) determine the effects of improving the operating conditions on the system's environmental im- pacts using LCA, including increasing the PL loading rate, using the biogas more efficiently, and replacing the generator and boiler with a combined heat and power (CHP) generator.

such as cow manure, grass, maize, and food waste (Beausang et al., 2020; Tsapekos et al., 2019; Van Stappen et al., 2016). There are not any comprehensive LCA studies for farm-scale AD systems using only PL as the substrate with nutrient capture and effluent recirculation (Van Stappen et al., 2016). Most previous LCA research has focused on municipal solid waste, sewage sludge, dairy manure, or food waste (Arena et al., 2003; Astrup et al., 2009; Cherubini et al., 2009). Only one prior study has used LCA on a farm-scale AD system for PL in Poland, but the system operated without nutrient capture or effluent recirculation (Beausang et al., 2020).
This study is the first to investigate a larger-scale AD system (400 m³

Fig. 1. The overview of the sampling points of the 22-month study shown in (a), with the life cycle (LCA) assessment boundary, inputs, and outputs for the anaerobic digestion (AD) and nutrient capture system shown in (b).

using PL-only with a novel system to capture the nutrient and the solids from the digestate and recirculate the liquid from the filter press while evaluating the system using LCA. The aims of this study were: 1) eval- uate the energy, nutrient, and organic transformations for a novel farm- scale AD design that digested only PL over a 22-month monitoring period; 2) determine the effect of recirculating ammonia-rich, recycled water from the filter press to increase the moisture of PL substrate feeding the AD system; 3) quantify the environmental impacts of an AD system with nutrient capture to produce energy from PL and extract nutrients from the PL matrix using LCA; and 4) determine the effects of improving the operating conditions on the system's environmental im- pacts using LCA, including increasing the PL loading rate, using the biogas more efficiently, and replacing the generator and boiler with a combined heat and power (CHP) generator.
7: Anaerobic digestion (AD) is a series of microbial processes to break down biodegradable material in the absence of oxygen producing biogas composed of 50-70% methane (CH4), CO2 (30-50%), water vapor (3-4 %), and hydrogen sulfide (H2S: 500-8000 ppm, <0.8%) (Appels et al., 2008; Labatut and Pronto, 2018). AD is gaining traction as a viable technique for mitigating greenhouse gas emissions and converting low- value materials to higher-value materials, which include biogas and fertilizer (Beausang et al., 2020). Biogas can be produced from a variety of organic materials, such as poultry litter (PL), dairy manure, food waste, crop waste, or sewage sludge (Achi et al., 2020; Appels et al., 2008; Deena et al., 2022). PL is a less common AD substrate compared to other manure substrates, with <10 operational AD systems in the United States (US) utilizing PL (USEPA, 2022). However, previous studies have reported that PL had higher CH4 potential than dairy manure (Achi et al., 2020; Itodo and Awulu, 1999).

The PL substrate consists of animal waste, feed residue, feathers, and bedding material, including wood chips, and is cleaned out of poultry houses between flocks (Hassanein et al., 2021). According to Singh et al. (2010), the lignin-rich bedding material is not readily digested by anaerobic bacteria due to the fact that lignin is not easily biodegraded, and furthermore, the lignin binds to cellulose and hemicellulose,

decreasing the availability of polysaccharides for hydrolysis (Abdel- Hamid et al., 2013; Shrestha et al., 2017). Additionally, PL has a low moisture content and a high ammonia-nitrogen content within a com- plex organic matrix. Successful AD of PL often requires the addition of water to the relatively dry (~20% moisture content) manure prior to AD processing (Dadrasnia et al., 2021). The total solids (TS) content is a critical parameter in AD of PL, impacting hydrolysis and methano- genesis rates (Indren et al., 2020). Abbassi-Guendouz et al. (2012) showed that the daily CH4 yield from PL was reduced by 60% when the TS content increased from 10 to 30 % due to slower hydrolysis rates and slower diffusion of soluble intermediate products at the higher TS level (Xu et al., 2014). Additionally, increasing the TS concentration from 5 to 20 % resulted in a sevenfold drop in CH4 output in ammonia-stressed digesters (Li et al., 2013). To overcome the dry nature of PL, the liquid effluent can be used to wet the incoming PL, but this effluent is often enriched in ammonia (NH). In the literature, most previous research on AD of PL has focused on batch systems (Achi et al., 2020; Hassanein et al., 2019; Indren et al., 2020).

The PL substrate and AD effluent contain a wide range of soluble metals and nutrients, including high NH4, which can impact AD (Möller and Müller, 2012). There is a lack of comprehensive knowledge on nutrient capture technologies post-AD. There has not been previous work focused on an integrated, continuous AD system for digesting only

PL with nutrient capture and recycling the digester effluent to the front of the AD system at the farm-scale. This was the first large-scale, recir- culating AD system utilizing only PL with no liquid leaving the system.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) can be used to determine environmental impacts while comparing different scenarios, including product inputs, outputs, and potential environmental effects throughout the life cycle (ISO 14040, 2006). LCAs do not take into account market characteris- tics, such as price impacts, supply, and demand (Choudhury et al., 2020; Yang and Heijungs, 2018). A LCA is considered a suitable tool to eval- uate the environmental sustainability of AD systems (Czyrnek-Delêtre et al., 2017) and has been used extensively to evaluate AD of feedstocks,

such as cow manure, grass, maize, and food waste (Beausang et al., 2020; Tsapekos et al., 2019; Van Stappen et al., 2016). There are not any comprehensive LCA studies for farm-scale AD systems using only PL as the substrate with nutrient capture and effluent recirculation (Van Stappen et al., 2016). Most previous LCA research has focused on municipal solid waste, sewage sludge, dairy manure, or food waste (Arena et al., 2003; Astrup et al., 2009; Cherubini et al., 2009). Only one prior study has used LCA on a farm-scale AD system for PL in Poland, but the system operated without nutrient capture or effluent recirculation (Beausang et al., 2020).
This study is the first to investigate a larger-scale AD system (400 m³

using PL-only with a novel system to capture the nutrient and the solids from the digestate and recirculate the liquid from the filter press while evaluating the system using LCA. The aims of this study were: 1) eval- uate the energy, nutrient, and organic transformations for a novel farm- scale AD design that digested only PL over a 22-month monitoring period; 2) determine the effect of recirculating ammonia-rich, recycled water from the filter press to increase the moisture of PL substrate feeding the AD system; 3) quantify the environmental impacts of an AD system with nutrient capture to produce energy from PL and extract nutrients from the PL matrix using LCA; and 4) determine the effects of improving the operating conditions on the system's environmental im- pacts using LCA, including increasing the PL loading rate, using the biogas more efficiently, and replacing the generator and boiler with a combined heat and power (CHP) generator.
8: The farm-scale AD system was located near Pocomoke City, Mary- land, USA and consisted of a mixing tank (39.6 two anaerobic di- gesters connected in-series, referred here as AD1 (212 m³ and AD2 (189 a H2S scrubber, a biogas boiler for system heating, a biogas- powered engine generator set for electricity production, and a nutrient capture system consisting of a solid separator followed by a filter press (Fig. 1). The operational procedure for digesting the PL consisted of the following steps: 1) the PL substrate was mixed with recycled water from the filter press in the mixing tank; 2) the PL and recirculation water mixture was added to AD1; 3) the effluent of AD1 flowed to AD2, with a target retention time of 30 days for the two digesters; 4) the AD2 effluent continued to a screw-press solid separator (Vinent KP-10, Tampa, Flor- ida, US), with the separated solids used as a fertilizer; 5) the liquid portion from the solid separator continued to a sulfuric acid tank for acidification prior to the first filter press cycle, and sodium hydroxide was added before the second filter press cycle; 6) the solids from the filter press were used as a filter cake fertilizer; 7) the liquid portion of the filter press was recycled to the mixing tank at the front of the AD system, with no liquid leaving the recirculating AD system; and 8) the biogas produced from the two digesters (AD1 and AD2) passed through three 250 L pelletized, iron oxide H2S scrubbers (Model PX-250, Shenzhen Puxin Technology Co. Ltd., Shenzhen, China) before use in a boiler for AD system heating, the engine generator for electricity generation, or flared. Back-up propane was used for AD heating when biogas was not available or the H2S concentration in the biogas was too high for boiler use (>10,000 ppm H2S). The scrubbers were designed for removing 5000 ppm H2S based on previous lab digester experiments. Likely due to the addition of the sulfuric acid in the system, H2S production was higher than expected, which overwhelmed the H2S scrubber system overtime, even with regular media replacement.

10: The project was evaluated over a 33-month period, with preliminary data collected for the first 11 months of the AD operation (preliminary data not shown), and the next 22 months presented in this study (referred to as Months 1-22). Only results from the last year of sampling (Months 11-22) were used in LCA. During the 22 months of sample collection, samples were collected monthly from 13 sampling points, which included 7 liquid samples, 4 solids samples, and 2 gas samples to track solids, organics, and nutrient transformations. An online moni- toring system measured the pH and temperature of the digesters, pro- pane usage, biogas produced, biogas flared, biogas used in the generator, electricity generated, and electricity use (Fig. 1). The authors designed the AD system and collected all the data presented here.

The initial loading of the 400 digestion system (AD1: 212 and AD2: 189 consisted of filling the digestion tanks with 35 °C water, adding 19 m³ dairy manure and 1.9 m³ PL digestate before starting the PL substrate loading. The system received 77.1 tons (TS) of PL in the first 6 months (data not shown), resulting in an organic loading rate (OLR) of ~0.75 kg volatile solids (VS) added/m3// Between Months 1-12, the average OLR was 0.64 kg VS added/m3 which increased in Months 13-22 to 0.88 kg VS added/m3/da

The initial loading of the 400 digestion system (AD1: 212 and AD2: 189 consisted of filling the digestion tanks with 35 °C water, adding 19 m³ dairy manure and 1.9 m³ PL digestate before starting the PL substrate loading. The system received 77.1 tons (TS) of PL in the first 6 months (data not shown), resulting in an organic loading rate (OLR) of ~0.75 kg volatile solids (VS) added/m3// Between Months 1-12, the average OLR was 0.64 kg VS added/m3 which increased in Months 13-22 to 0.88 kg VS added/m3/da
12: The solid and liquid samples were analyzed for TS, VS, chemical oxygen demand (COD), soluble COD (sCOD), volatile fatty acids (VFAs), pH, total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN), total phosphorus (TP), and ammo- nium-nitrogen (NH4-N). The biogas was analyzed for quantity and quality, including the CH4, H2S, and CO2 content in the biogas. All samples were analyzed in triplicate and followed laboratory quality control and quality assurance procedures. The pH was measured using a glass electrode Accumet AB 15 pH meter. Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater (Eaton, 2017) was used to determine TS (Method 2540B) and VS (Method 2540 E). The COD and sCOD concentrations were measured in liquid samples using the diges- tion method adapted by HACH (Jirka and Carter, 1975), with sCOD filtered through a 0.45 um membrane prior to analysis. Due to the high solids content of the PL substrate, only sCOD was measured from Months 8 to 22 of the study. Ammonia samples were determined for liquid samples after acidification with 15 N sulfuric acid to pH 2, filtration through a 0.45 um membrane, and analyzed using a Lachat instrument (QuikChem Method 10-107-06-2-0). The TKN and TP of liquid and solid samples were digested with 15 N sulfuric acid and CuSO45H20, filtered through a 0.45 um membrane filter, and analyzed on the Lachat (QuikChem Method 13-107-06-2-D for TKN and QuikChem Method 13- 115-01-1-B for TP). For VFA determination, acidified (pH 2.0) liquid samples were centrifuged for 20 min at 5000 RPM, the supernatant was filtered to 0.22 um, and the liquid filtrate was analyzed for VFAs (ace- tate, propionic, valeric, and butyric acids) using a 7890A Agilent gas chromatograph. An automated system measured the temperature (Acromag ST-132, Wixom, Michigan, US) and pH (Sensorex DA650CD, Garden Grove, California, US) inside AD1 and AD2 at one-minute intervals.
13: Biogas samples were analyzed using a handheld Landtec Biogas 5000 before and after the H2S scrubbing system, with the readings verified throughout the year using a 7890A Agilent gas chromatograph, as described in our previous research (Achi et al., 2020). The facility was equipped with a biogas analyzer (MRU SWG100, Neckarsulm, Germany) that measured the CH4, CO2, and H2S every 2 min before and after the H2S scrubbing system. The biogas quantity was measured using a pulse gas meter (1 pulse per 0.0283 m³ (EKM-PGM 75, California, USA) equipped with a data logger (Omnimeter Pulse v.4, California, USA), with the data sent to an online system every 15 min.
14: The LCA was conducted using SimaPro software developed by PRé Consultants (Amersfoort, Utrecht, Netherlands). The scope of the anal- ysis was "from cradle to grave," including material manufacturing to disposal. The LCA methodology was performed according to the ISO 14040, which includes the goal and scope definition (ISO 14041), in- ventory analysis (ISO 14041), impact assessment (ISO 14042), and interpretation (ISO 14043). The calculations allowed for the inputs, outputs, and environmental impacts to be presented based on a

functional unit of 1 ton of PL added to the system (Fig. 1). Sensitivity analyses were conducted to evaluate how doubling or tripling the rele- vant parameters affected the impact for each category tested. Ten impact categories were included in the LCA: global warming, ozone depletion, smog, acidification, eutrophication, carcinogens, non-carcinogens, res- piratory effects, ecotoxicity, and fossil fuel depletion. The LCA analysis included poultry litter storage, transportation, energy production, en- ergy use, the two digesters, the nutrient capture system components, operational parameters, and the fertilizer produced. The impacts of manure production were not included in the study, as poultry is bred for meat and eggs, not for the manure.

Eight scenarios were analyzed (Table 1) based on the use of the biogas in the flare, generator, boiler (project current scenario), or CHP (future scenario) and compared to the operating conditions of the AD and nutrient capture system using the average values from the last year of data collection (Months 11-22) in Scenario A and the highest CH4 production data (Months 20-22) in Scenarios B, C, D, and E. A H2S scrubber efficiency of 100 was assumed for Scenarios A, B, C, D, and E. Scenario A used 100 % of the produced biogas in the generator for electricity production. Scenario B used 100 % of the produced biogas in the boiler for heating the digestion system, and Scenario C used 100' % of the biogas in a CHP for electricity generation and heating the digesters. Scenarios D and E increased the amount of poultry litter fed to the system (twice and triple, respectively) while using 100 % of biogas in a CHP system for electricity generation and AD heating instead of using biogas in flare, generator, and boiler (current scenario). Currently, the state of Maryland (MD) in the US has a PL transport program due to the high soil P concentrations in MD, especially on the Eastern Shore of Maryland, to move PL to other areas of the state (or out of state) to agricultural lands with less soil P concentrations (MDA, 2020). All sce- narios were compared to a Baseline Scenario, which transported the unprocessed PL off the Eastern Shore of MD (40 km one-way, 80 km roundtrip). All Scenarios were run using three transportation distances for the PL (0, 40, and 80 km roundtrip) to assess the effect of PL trans- portation on process sustainability.

16: The biogas and CH4 production data (per kg VS) and H2S

concentration in the biogas were analyzed using a t-test, with p-values <0.05 considered significant. The TS, VS, VFAs, COD, TKN, TP, and NH3 were analyzed for significant differences (p-values <0.05) using two- way ANOVA with Tukey's honestly significant difference (HSD) test performed to determine significant differences between AD influent, AD effluent, and after the filter press, as well as differences between monthly data points. The LCA environmental impact for all the ten impact categories associated with the eight scenarios tested in this study was analyzed for significant differences using analysis t-test with p- values <0.05. All the statistics analysis were done using StatPlus (mac- Version v7, AnalystSoft Inc., USA) (Hijazi et al., 2020).
17: An average of 11.2 + 1.2 tons/month (122.3 tons/year; 84.6 tons dry weight (DW)/year) of PL were processed in the AD system, with an average TS and VS content of the unprocessed PL feedstock of 70.2 + 2.6 % and 53.6 + 1.96 %, respectively. Prior to AD, the PL substrate was mixed with recycled water (147 /month) and occasionally with well water (0.41 m³ /month). The ratio of recycled water to PL was 15.7 m³ recycled water/ton of PL processed. The average energy production from the AD system was 677 m³ biogas/month, with an average of 64 CH4 in the biogas (430 m³ CH4/month; Fig. 2).
18: The biogas production had average CH4 concentrations slightly higher (64 %) than the range reported in other PL digestion studies (58-62%) (Bayrakdar et al., 2018; Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019). The average normalized CH4 production was 112 L CH4/kg VS but rose to 222 L CH4/kg VS during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) of the study period (Fig. 2). The CH4 production during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) was significantly higher than in Months 1-3 and Months 17-19 (p-values = 0.0179 and 0.0318, respectively). Previous research on PL digestion has shown digestion efficiency values ranging from 150 to 300 L CH4/kg VS (Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019; Hassanein et al., 2019). The average CH4 production in this sys- tem (112 L CH4/kg VS) was below this range, likely due to non- acclimatized microbes during the AD startup. The average H2S concentration was 14,458 ppm before the H2S scrubber and 7597 ppm after the scrubber (Fig. 2) (p-value <0.0001), resulting in an average H2S removal efficacy of 47.6 with >85.0 % H2S removal efficiency during Months 1-4. The H2S scrubber efficiency decreased with the increase in the H2S concentration (>10,000 ppm) in the raw biogas over a 16-month period (Months 5-15) (Fig. 2), showing H2S scrubber failure, which was remedied after this present study concluded by switching out the media and increasing microaeration within the AD system.

The H2S content in the biogas produced from PL digestion can be as high as 30,000 ppm, which can inhibit CH4 production and requires efficient scrubbing equipment to purify the biogas before it use in an engine generator, boiler, or fuel cell systems (Yan et al., 2018). Scrub- bing of H2S from the biogas is recommended prior to using the biogas in a generator or boiler, as H2S is a highly corrosive and hazardous gas (Shelford et al., 2019). The H2S concentrations in the raw biogas from this study were all above the recommended maximum H2S concentra- tion for biogas end use in a boiler (1000 ppm), engine-generator set (500 ppm), vehicle fuel (23 ppm), pipeline injection (4 ppm), and fuel cell (1 ppm) (Shelford et al., 2019).
3.2. Total solids (TS) and volatile solids (VS) concentrations
When the percentage of the VS in an AD system increases, there can

be a corresponding increase in the produced biogas. However, if the biological capacity of organisms in the digester to process the VS is exceeded, the pH can decrease from excessive VFA formation, resulting in a reduction in biogas production. In this system, the AD1 influent TS significantly increased over time from 2.59 % (month 1) to 5.73 % (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001), with an average influent TS of 2.92% (Fig. 3). The average influent VS content was .6%, increasing from 0.9 (Month 1) to 3.59% VS (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001); Fig. 3). The TS and VS were significantly reduced from the AD1 influent to the AD2 effluent by 30.9 and 35.9 % (p-values <0.0001), respectively, as the organic matter was converted to CH4. With solids removed in the screw press separator and filter press, the total reduction in TS and VS from the AD1 influent to the recycled water effluent was 48.1 and 57.6 %, respectively (p-values <0.0001 and 0.0005, respectively) (Fig. 3).

During the final year of monitoring for this study (Months 11-22), the cumulative solids produced from eight runs of the nutrient capture system were 6.2 tons of separated solids (2.1 tons DW), 15.6 tons of solids from the first cycle of the filter press (6.0 tons DW), and 1.1 tons of solids from the second cycle of the filter press (0.5 tons DW). All the recycled water (averaging 147 m³ /month) was recovered from the filter press, stored, and then mixed with the PL and additional well water (averaging 0.41 m³ /month) to feed the AD system, with no liquid dis- charged from the AD system; only the three solid products left the sys- tem as a fertilizer source.
3.3. Total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN), total phosphorus (TP), and ammonium-nitrogen (NH4-N) concentrations

Unprocessed PL is often directly applied to cropland to take advan- tage of its high nutrient content. But in some poultry producing regions, such as the Chesapeake Bay region in the MidAtlantic US, over- application of manure has led to high soil phosphorus content, with runoff from those areas contributing to eutrophication of the Ches- apeake Bay (Liu et al., 2018). In the 22-month monitoring period, the average of TKN and TP of the unprocessed PL were 37,239 mg N/kg, and 15,305 mg P/kg, respectively (Fig. 4). The average concentrations of TKN and TP in the recycled water were 2931 mg N/L, and 192 mg P/L, respectively, resulting in the average AD1 influent concentration for the combined PL and recycled water to be 3014 mg N/L and 415 mg P/L, respectively, with the high nutrient value in the diluted substrate due to the nutrients still present in the recycled water (Fig. 4). The AD1 influent TKN concentrations in the mixing tank increased by 133 % from Month (1739 mg N/L) to Month 22 (4069 mg N/L) (p-value = 0.0402), as the VS loading rate to the digester increased and the use of recirculated water increased the nutrient content overtime in the influent material (Fig. 5). The AD1 TP concentrations increased in the mixing tank by 54.5% from Month 1 (231 mg P/L) to Month 22 (357 mg P/L) (p-value 0.172), but this increase was not significant as most of the P remained in the solids, which were removed from the system through solids sep- aration and the filter press before recycling the water to the front of the system (Figs. 4 and 5).

The solids separator and filter press were operated intermittently during the monitoring period. From Months 11 to 22, 122 tons of PL (84.6 tons DW, consisting of 3.7 tons TKN and 1.9 tons TP) were added to the AD and nutrient capture system mixed with 147 m³ recycled water/month and 0.41 m³ well water/month. The recycled water con- tained solids and nutrients, resulting in 2.9 tons DW solids/month in the recycled water containing 0.44 tons TKN/month and 0.03 tons TP/ month. The cumulative solids collected from the eight runs of the solid separator were 6220 kg (2126 kg DW, containing 41.2 kg TKN and 19.7 kg TP). The cumulative filter cake solids collected from the first run of the filter press were 15,630 kg (6220 kg DW, containing 83 kg TKN and 40 kg TP), with 1.1 tons of filter cake collected from the second cycle of the filter press over the eight runs (529 kg DW, containing 8.7 kg TKN, and 9.3 kg TP). The average TKN concentration in the separated solids was 19,394 mg N/kg DW, and the average TP concentration was 9270

After the solids separator and before the filter press, an acid tank was used to strip ammonia from the liquid phase to the solid phase, as an ammonium sulfate solid, to increase the amount of NH4 in the filter cake fertilizer and reduce the NH4 concentration remaining in the recycle water (Törnwall et al., 2017). During the final year of monitoring (Months 11-22), there was 273 kg of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) added to the

acid tank, and 82 kg of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) added to the liquid fraction after the first cycle of the filter press. Previous studies have shown that H2SO4 addition can remove between 40 and 80 % of NH4 from the liquid, depending on the quantity of H2SO4 added (Guo et al., 2009; Jurgutis et al., 2020; Törnwall et al., 2017). A H2SO4 input also dissolves calcium phosphate in the substrate, which increases the phosphate solubility. The increase in the SO concentration in the liquid likely led to the increase in the H2S concentration over time (Fig. 2; Olivera et al., 2022). When the pH is increased through NaOH after the first cycle of the filter press, the P can precipitate as struvite in the filter cake produced from the second cycle of the filter press (Szogi

Fig. 5. The nutrients in the AD1 influent, AD2 effluent, and recycled water from the filter press to the front of the AD system, with the total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN) shown in (a), the total phosphorus (TP) shown in (b), and the total ammonium nitrogen (NH4-N) concentration (mg/L) shown in (c). The TKN and TP concentrations of the solid products from the solid separator and second cycle of the filter press from Months 3-22 are shown in (d).

The NH4 concentration increased 407 % in the AD1 influent from Month 1 (550 mg/L) to Month 22 (2793 mg/L) (p-value = 0.0001) due to the high NH4 concentration in the recycled water, which increased from 741 mg/L (Month 1) to 2921 mg (Month 22) (p-value <0.0001), with a high average NH4-N concentration in the AD1 effluent (2037 mg/ L) and AD2 effluent (1821 mg/L). There was no evidence of NH3 toxicity in the AD system, but the pH did increase in the recycling the water from 7.1 (Month 1) to 8.2 (Month 22) (Fig. 5).

The digestion process is sensitive to pH, with the biological processes having a pH range of 6.6-7.8 for optimal CH4 production (Eryildiz et al., 2020). The pH in the AD system increased from 7.6 to 8.2 after more than 2 years of recycling the water to the front of the system, with a pH 7.8 higher considered above the optimal pH range (6.6-7.8) for CH4 production (Fu et al., 2021). The increase in pH was likely related to the increase in NH4-N concentration in the recycled water (Fig. 4). In Month 20, the biogas production was at a peak when the pH dropped below 8.0 to 7.9, and then the biogas production decreased in Months 21 and 22 when the pH was >8.0 (Fig. 6).
19: The biogas production had average CH4 concentrations slightly higher (64 %) than the range reported in other PL digestion studies (58-62%) (Bayrakdar et al., 2018; Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019). The average normalized CH4 production was 112 L CH4/kg VS but rose to 222 L CH4/kg VS during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) of the study period (Fig. 2). The CH4 production during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) was significantly higher than in Months 1-3 and Months 17-19 (p-values = 0.0179 and 0.0318, respectively). Previous research on PL digestion has shown digestion efficiency values ranging from 150 to 300 L CH4/kg VS (Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019; Hassanein et al., 2019). The average CH4 production in this sys- tem (112 L CH4/kg VS) was below this range, likely due to non- acclimatized microbes during the AD startup. The average H2S concentration was 14,458 ppm before the H2S scrubber and 7597 ppm after the scrubber (Fig. 2) (p-value <0.0001), resulting in an average H2S removal efficacy of 47.6 with >85.0 % H2S removal efficiency during Months 1-4. The H2S scrubber efficiency decreased with the increase in the H2S concentration (>10,000 ppm) in the raw biogas over a 16-month period (Months 5-15) (Fig. 2), showing H2S scrubber failure, which was remedied after this present study concluded by switching out the media and increasing microaeration within the AD system.

The H2S content in the biogas produced from PL digestion can be as high as 30,000 ppm, which can inhibit CH4 production and requires efficient scrubbing equipment to purify the biogas before it use in an engine generator, boiler, or fuel cell systems (Yan et al., 2018). Scrub- bing of H2S from the biogas is recommended prior to using the biogas in a generator or boiler, as H2S is a highly corrosive and hazardous gas (Shelford et al., 2019). The H2S concentrations in the raw biogas from this study were all above the recommended maximum H2S concentra- tion for biogas end use in a boiler (1000 ppm), engine-generator set (500 ppm), vehicle fuel (23 ppm), pipeline injection (4 ppm), and fuel cell (1 ppm) (Shelford et al., 2019).
3.2. Total solids (TS) and volatile solids (VS) concentrations
When the percentage of the VS in an AD system increases, there can

be a corresponding increase in the produced biogas. However, if the biological capacity of organisms in the digester to process the VS is exceeded, the pH can decrease from excessive VFA formation, resulting in a reduction in biogas production. In this system, the AD1 influent TS significantly increased over time from 2.59 % (month 1) to 5.73 % (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001), with an average influent TS of 2.92% (Fig. 3). The average influent VS content was .6%, increasing from 0.9 (Month 1) to 3.59% VS (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001); Fig. 3). The TS and VS were significantly reduced from the AD1 influent to the AD2 effluent by 30.9 and 35.9 % (p-values <0.0001), respectively, as the organic matter was converted to CH4. With solids removed in the screw press separator and filter press, the total reduction in TS and VS from the AD1 influent to the recycled water effluent was 48.1 and 57.6 %, respectively (p-values <0.0001 and 0.0005, respectively) (Fig. 3).

During the final year of monitoring for this study (Months 11-22), the cumulative solids produced from eight runs of the nutrient capture system were 6.2 tons of separated solids (2.1 tons DW), 15.6 tons of solids from the first cycle of the filter press (6.0 tons DW), and 1.1 tons of solids from the second cycle of the filter press (0.5 tons DW). All the recycled water (averaging 147 m³ /month) was recovered from the filter press, stored, and then mixed with the PL and additional well water (averaging 0.41 m³ /month) to feed the AD system, with no liquid dis- charged from the AD system; only the three solid products left the sys- tem as a fertilizer source.
3.3. Total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN), total phosphorus (TP), and ammonium-nitrogen (NH4-N) concentrations

Unprocessed PL is often directly applied to cropland to take advan- tage of its high nutrient content. But in some poultry producing regions, such as the Chesapeake Bay region in the MidAtlantic US, over- application of manure has led to high soil phosphorus content, with runoff from those areas contributing to eutrophication of the Ches- apeake Bay (Liu et al., 2018). In the 22-month monitoring period, the average of TKN and TP of the unprocessed PL were 37,239 mg N/kg, and 15,305 mg P/kg, respectively (Fig. 4). The average concentrations of TKN and TP in the recycled water were 2931 mg N/L, and 192 mg P/L, respectively, resulting in the average AD1 influent concentration for the combined PL and recycled water to be 3014 mg N/L and 415 mg P/L, respectively, with the high nutrient value in the diluted substrate due to the nutrients still present in the recycled water (Fig. 4). The AD1 influent TKN concentrations in the mixing tank increased by 133 % from Month (1739 mg N/L) to Month 22 (4069 mg N/L) (p-value = 0.0402), as the VS loading rate to the digester increased and the use of recirculated water increased the nutrient content overtime in the influent material (Fig. 5). After the solids separator and before the filter press, an acid tank was used to strip ammonia from the liquid phase to the solid phase, as an ammonium sulfate solid, to increase the amount of NH4 in the filter cake fertilizer and reduce the NH4 concentration remaining in the recycle water (Törnwall et al., 2017). During the final year of monitoring (Months 11-22), there was 273 kg of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) added to the

acid tank, and 82 kg of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) added to the liquid fraction after the first cycle of the filter press. Previous studies have shown that H2SO4 addition can remove between 40 and 80 % of NH4 from the liquid, depending on the quantity of H2SO4 added (Guo et al., 2009; Jurgutis et al., 2020; Törnwall et al., 2017). A H2SO4 input also dissolves calcium phosphate in the substrate, which increases the phosphate solubility. The increase in the SO concentration in the liquid likely led to the increase in the H2S concentration over time (Fig. 2; Olivera et al., 2022). When the pH is increased through NaOH after the first cycle of the filter press, the P can precipitate as struvite in the filter cake produced from the second cycle of the filter press (Szogi
Fig. 4. The mass and nutrient balance of the system with the poultry litter (PL) substrate, well water, and recycled water added and solids removed from the system.

Fig. 5. The nutrients in the AD1 influent, AD2 effluent, and recycled water from the filter press to the front of the AD system, with the total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN) shown in (a), the total phosphorus (TP) shown in (b), and the total ammonium nitrogen (NH4-N) concentration (mg/L) shown in (c). The TKN and TP concentrations of the solid products from the solid separator and second cycle of the filter press from Months 3-22 are shown in (d).

The NH4 concentration increased 407 % in the AD1 influent from Month 1 (550 mg/L) to Month 22 (2793 mg/L) (p-value = 0.0001) due to the high NH4 concentration in the recycled water, which increased from 741 mg/L (Month 1) to 2921 mg (Month 22) (p-value <0.0001), with a high average NH4-N concentration in the AD1 effluent (2037 mg/ L) and AD2 effluent (1821 mg/L). There was no evidence of NH3 toxicity in the AD system, but the pH did increase in the recycling the water from 7.1 (Month 1) to 8.2 (Month 22) (Fig. 5).

The digestion process is sensitive to pH, with the biological processes having a pH range of 6.6-7.8 for optimal CH4 production (Eryildiz et al., 2020). The pH in the AD system increased from 7.6 to 8.2 after more than 2 years of recycling the water to the front of the system, with a pH 7.8 higher considered above the optimal pH range (6.6-7.8) for CH4 production (Fu et al., 2021). The increase in pH was likely related to the increase in NH4-N concentration in the recycled water (Fig. 4). In Month 20, the biogas production was at a peak when the pH dropped below 8.0 to 7.9, and then the biogas production decreased in Months 21 and 22 when the pH was >8.0 (Fig. 6).
21: The biogas production had average CH4 concentrations slightly higher (64 %) than the range reported in other PL digestion studies (58-62%) (Bayrakdar et al., 2018; Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019). The average normalized CH4 production was 112 L CH4/kg VS but rose to 222 L CH4/kg VS during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) of the study period (Fig. 2). The CH4 production during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) was significantly higher than in Months 1-3 and Months 17-19 (p-values = 0.0179 and 0.0318, respectively). Previous research on PL digestion has shown digestion efficiency values ranging from 150 to 300 L CH4/kg VS (Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019; Hassanein et al., 2019). The average CH4 production in this sys- tem (112 L CH4/kg VS) was below this range, likely due to non- acclimatized microbes during the AD startup. The average H2S concentration was 14,458 ppm before the H2S scrubber and 7597 ppm after the scrubber (Fig. 2) (p-value <0.0001), resulting in an average H2S removal efficacy of 47.6 with >85.0 % H2S removal efficiency during Months 1-4. The H2S scrubber efficiency decreased with the increase in the H2S concentration (>10,000 ppm) in the raw biogas over a 16-month period (Months 5-15) (Fig. 2), showing H2S scrubber failure, which was remedied after this present study concluded by switching out the media and increasing microaeration within the AD system.

The H2S content in the biogas produced from PL digestion can be as high as 30,000 ppm, which can inhibit CH4 production and requires efficient scrubbing equipment to purify the biogas before it use in an engine generator, boiler, or fuel cell systems (Yan et al., 2018). Scrub- bing of H2S from the biogas is recommended prior to using the biogas in a generator or boiler, as H2S is a highly corrosive and hazardous gas (Shelford et al., 2019). The H2S concentrations in the raw biogas from this study were all above the recommended maximum H2S concentra- tion for biogas end use in a boiler (1000 ppm), engine-generator set (500 ppm), vehicle fuel (23 ppm), pipeline injection (4 ppm), and fuel cell (1 ppm) (Shelford et al., 2019).
3.2. Total solids (TS) and volatile solids (VS) concentrations
When the percentage of the VS in an AD system increases, there can

be a corresponding increase in the produced biogas. However, if the biological capacity of organisms in the digester to process the VS is exceeded, the pH can decrease from excessive VFA formation, resulting in a reduction in biogas production. In this system, the AD1 influent TS significantly increased over time from 2.59 % (month 1) to 5.73 % (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001), with an average influent TS of 2.92% (Fig. 3). The average influent VS content was .6%, increasing from 0.9 (Month 1) to 3.59% VS (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001); Fig. 3). The TS and VS were significantly reduced from the AD1 influent to the AD2 effluent by 30.9 and 35.9 % (p-values <0.0001), respectively, as the organic matter was converted to CH4. With solids removed in the screw press separator and filter press, the total reduction in TS and VS from the AD1 influent to the recycled water effluent was 48.1 and 57.6 %, respectively (p-values <0.0001 and 0.0005, respectively) (Fig. 3).

During the final year of monitoring for this study (Months 11-22), the cumulative solids produced from eight runs of the nutrient capture system were 6.2 tons of separated solids (2.1 tons DW), 15.6 tons of solids from the first cycle of the filter press (6.0 tons DW), and 1.1 tons of solids from the second cycle of the filter press (0.5 tons DW). All the recycled water (averaging 147 m³ /month) was recovered from the filter press, stored, and then mixed with the PL and additional well water (averaging 0.41 m³ /month) to feed the AD system, with no liquid dis- charged from the AD system; only the three solid products left the sys- tem as a fertilizer source.
3.3. Total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN), total phosphorus (TP), and ammonium-nitrogen (NH4-N) concentrations

Unprocessed PL is often directly applied to cropland to take advan- tage of its high nutrient content. But in some poultry producing regions, such as the Chesapeake Bay region in the MidAtlantic US, over- application of manure has led to high soil phosphorus content, with runoff from those areas contributing to eutrophication of the Ches- apeake Bay (Liu et al., 2018). In the 22-month monitoring period, the average of TKN and TP of the unprocessed PL were 37,239 mg N/kg, and 15,305 mg P/kg, respectively (Fig. 4). The average concentrations of TKN and TP in the recycled water were 2931 mg N/L, and 192 mg P/L, respectively, resulting in the average AD1 influent concentration for the combined PL and recycled water to be 3014 mg N/L and 415 mg P/L, respectively, with the high nutrient value in the diluted substrate due to the nutrients still present in the recycled water (Fig. 4). The AD1 influent TKN concentrations in the mixing tank increased by 133 % from Month (1739 mg N/L) to Month 22 (4069 mg N/L) (p-value = 0.0402), as the VS loading rate to the digester increased and the use of recirculated water increased the nutrient content overtime in the influent material (Fig. 5). The AD1 TP concentrations increased in the mixing tank by 54.5% from Month 1 (231 mg P/L) to Month 22 (357 mg P/L) (p-value 0.172), but this increase was not significant as most of the P remained in the solids, which were removed from the system through solids sep- aration and the filter press before recycling the water to the front of the system (Figs. 4 and 5).

The solids separator and filter press were operated intermittently during the monitoring period. From Months 11 to 22, 122 tons of PL (84.6 tons DW, consisting of 3.7 tons TKN and 1.9 tons TP) were added to the AD and nutrient capture system mixed with 147 m³ recycled water/month and 0.41 m³ well water/month. The recycled water con- tained solids and nutrients, resulting in 2.9 tons DW solids/month in the recycled water containing 0.44 tons TKN/month and 0.03 tons TP/ month. The cumulative solids collected from the eight runs of the solid separator were 6220 kg (2126 kg DW, containing 41.2 kg TKN and 19.7 kg TP). The cumulative filter cake solids collected from the first run of the filter press were 15,630 kg (6220 kg DW, containing 83 kg TKN and 40 kg TP), with 1.1 tons of filter cake collected from the second cycle of the filter press over the eight runs (529 kg DW, containing 8.7 kg TKN, and 9.3 kg TP). The average TKN concentration in the separated solids was 19,394 mg N/kg DW, and the average TP concentration was 9270

After the solids separator and before the filter press, an acid tank was used to strip ammonia from the liquid phase to the solid phase, as an ammonium sulfate solid, to increase the amount of NH4 in the filter cake fertilizer and reduce the NH4 concentration remaining in the recycle water (Törnwall et al., 2017). During the final year of monitoring (Months 11-22), there was 273 kg of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) added to the

acid tank, and 82 kg of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) added to the liquid fraction after the first cycle of the filter press. Previous studies have shown that H2SO4 addition can remove between 40 and 80 % of NH4 from the liquid, depending on the quantity of H2SO4 added (Guo et al., 2009; Jurgutis et al., 2020; Törnwall et al., 2017). A H2SO4 input also dissolves calcium phosphate in the substrate, which increases the phosphate solubility. The increase in the SO concentration in the liquid likely led to the increase in the H2S concentration over time (Fig. 2; Olivera et al., 2022). When the pH is increased through NaOH after the first cycle of the filter press, the P can precipitate as struvite in the filter cake produced from the second cycle of the filter press (Szogi

Fig. 5. The nutrients in the AD1 influent, AD2 effluent, and recycled water from the filter press to the front of the AD system, with the total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN) shown in (a), the total phosphorus (TP) shown in (b), and the total ammonium nitrogen (NH4-N) concentration (mg/L) shown in (c). The TKN and TP concentrations of the solid products from the solid separator and second cycle of the filter press from Months 3-22 are shown in (d).

The NH4 concentration increased 407 % in the AD1 influent from Month 1 (550 mg/L) to Month 22 (2793 mg/L) (p-value = 0.0001) due to the high NH4 concentration in the recycled water, which increased from 741 mg/L (Month 1) to 2921 mg (Month 22) (p-value <0.0001), with a high average NH4-N concentration in the AD1 effluent (2037 mg/ L) and AD2 effluent (1821 mg/L). There was no evidence of NH3 toxicity in the AD system, but the pH did increase in the recycling the water from 7.1 (Month 1) to 8.2 (Month 22) (Fig. 5).

The digestion process is sensitive to pH, with the biological processes having a pH range of 6.6-7.8 for optimal CH4 production (Eryildiz et al., 2020). The pH in the AD system increased from 7.6 to 8.2 after more than 2 years of recycling the water to the front of the system, with a pH 7.8 higher considered above the optimal pH range (6.6-7.8) for CH4 production (Fu et al., 2021). The increase in pH was likely related to the increase in NH4-N concentration in the recycled water (Fig. 4). In Month 20, the biogas production was at a peak when the pH dropped below 8.0 to 7.9, and then the biogas production decreased in Months 21 and 22 when the pH was >8.0 (Fig. 6).
23: The biogas production had average CH4 concentrations slightly higher (64 %) than the range reported in other PL digestion studies (58-62%) (Bayrakdar et al., 2018; Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019). The average normalized CH4 production was 112 L CH4/kg VS but rose to 222 L CH4/kg VS during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) of the study period (Fig. 2). The CH4 production during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) was significantly higher than in Months 1-3 and Months 17-19 (p-values = 0.0179 and 0.0318, respectively). Previous research on PL digestion has shown digestion efficiency values ranging from 150 to 300 L CH4/kg VS (Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019; Hassanein et al., 2019). The average CH4 production in this sys- tem (112 L CH4/kg VS) was below this range, likely due to non- acclimatized microbes during the AD startup. The average H2S concentration was 14,458 ppm before the H2S scrubber and 7597 ppm after the scrubber (Fig. 2) (p-value <0.0001), resulting in an average H2S removal efficacy of 47.6 with >85.0 % H2S removal efficiency during Months 1-4. The H2S scrubber efficiency decreased with the increase in the H2S concentration (>10,000 ppm) in the raw biogas over a 16-month period (Months 5-15) (Fig. 2), showing H2S scrubber failure, which was remedied after this present study concluded by switching out the media and increasing microaeration within the AD system.

The H2S content in the biogas produced from PL digestion can be as high as 30,000 ppm, which can inhibit CH4 production and requires efficient scrubbing equipment to purify the biogas before it use in an engine generator, boiler, or fuel cell systems (Yan et al., 2018). Scrub- bing of H2S from the biogas is recommended prior to using the biogas in a generator or boiler, as H2S is a highly corrosive and hazardous gas (Shelford et al., 2019). The H2S concentrations in the raw biogas from this study were all above the recommended maximum H2S concentra- tion for biogas end use in a boiler (1000 ppm), engine-generator set (500 ppm), vehicle fuel (23 ppm), pipeline injection (4 ppm), and fuel cell (1 ppm) (Shelford et al., 2019).
3.2. Total solids (TS) and volatile solids (VS) concentrations
When the percentage of the VS in an AD system increases, there can

be a corresponding increase in the produced biogas. However, if the biological capacity of organisms in the digester to process the VS is exceeded, the pH can decrease from excessive VFA formation, resulting in a reduction in biogas production. In this system, the AD1 influent TS significantly increased over time from 2.59 % (month 1) to 5.73 % (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001), with an average influent TS of 2.92% (Fig. 3). The average influent VS content was .6%, increasing from 0.9 (Month 1) to 3.59% VS (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001); Fig. 3). The TS and VS were significantly reduced from the AD1 influent to the AD2 effluent by 30.9 and 35.9 % (p-values <0.0001), respectively, as the organic matter was converted to CH4. With solids removed in the screw press separator and filter press, the total reduction in TS and VS from the AD1 influent to the recycled water effluent was 48.1 and 57.6 %, respectively (p-values <0.0001 and 0.0005, respectively) (Fig. 3).

During the final year of monitoring for this study (Months 11-22), the cumulative solids produced from eight runs of the nutrient capture system were 6.2 tons of separated solids (2.1 tons DW), 15.6 tons of solids from the first cycle of the filter press (6.0 tons DW), and 1.1 tons of solids from the second cycle of the filter press (0.5 tons DW). All the recycled water (averaging 147 m³ /month) was recovered from the filter press, stored, and then mixed with the PL and additional well water (averaging 0.41 m³ /month) to feed the AD system, with no liquid dis- charged from the AD system; only the three solid products left the sys- tem as a fertilizer source.
3.3. Total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN), total phosphorus (TP), and ammonium-nitrogen (NH4-N) concentrations

Unprocessed PL is often directly applied to cropland to take advan- tage of its high nutrient content. But in some poultry producing regions, such as the Chesapeake Bay region in the MidAtlantic US, over- application of manure has led to high soil phosphorus content, with runoff from those areas contributing to eutrophication of the Ches- apeake Bay (Liu et al., 2018). In the 22-month monitoring period, the average of TKN and TP of the unprocessed PL were 37,239 mg N/kg, and 15,305 mg P/kg, respectively (Fig. 4). The average concentrations of TKN and TP in the recycled water were 2931 mg N/L, and 192 mg P/L, respectively, resulting in the average AD1 influent concentration for the combined PL and recycled water to be 3014 mg N/L and 415 mg P/L, respectively, with the high nutrient value in the diluted substrate due to the nutrients still present in the recycled water (Fig. 4). The AD1 influent TKN concentrations in the mixing tank increased by 133 % from Month (1739 mg N/L) to Month 22 (4069 mg N/L) (p-value = 0.0402), as the VS loading rate to the digester increased and the use of recirculated water increased the nutrient content overtime in the influent material (Fig. 5). The AD1 TP concentrations increased in the mixing tank by 54.5% from Month 1 (231 mg P/L) to Month 22 (357 mg P/L) (p-value 0.172), but this increase was not significant as most of the P remained in the solids, which were removed from the system through solids sep- aration and the filter press before recycling the water to the front of the system (Figs. 4 and 5).

The solids separator and filter press were operated intermittently during the monitoring period. From Months 11 to 22, 122 tons of PL (84.6 tons DW, consisting of 3.7 tons TKN and 1.9 tons TP) were added to the AD and nutrient capture system mixed with 147 m³ recycled water/month and 0.41 m³ well water/month. The recycled water con- tained solids and nutrients, resulting in 2.9 tons DW solids/month in the recycled water containing 0.44 tons TKN/month and 0.03 tons TP/ month. The cumulative solids collected from the eight runs of the solid separator were 6220 kg (2126 kg DW, containing 41.2 kg TKN and 19.7 kg TP). The cumulative filter cake solids collected from the first run of the filter press were 15,630 kg (6220 kg DW, containing 83 kg TKN and 40 kg TP), with 1.1 tons of filter cake collected from the second cycle of the filter press over the eight runs (529 kg DW, containing 8.7 kg TKN, and 9.3 kg TP). The average TKN concentration in the separated solids was 19,394 mg N/kg DW, and the average TP concentration was 9270

After the solids separator and before the filter press, an acid tank was used to strip ammonia from the liquid phase to the solid phase, as an ammonium sulfate solid, to increase the amount of NH4 in the filter cake fertilizer and reduce the NH4 concentration remaining in the recycle water (Törnwall et al., 2017). During the final year of monitoring (Months 11-22), there was 273 kg of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) added to the

acid tank, and 82 kg of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) added to the liquid fraction after the first cycle of the filter press. Previous studies have shown that H2SO4 addition can remove between 40 and 80 % of NH4 from the liquid, depending on the quantity of H2SO4 added (Guo et al., 2009; Jurgutis et al., 2020; Törnwall et al., 2017). A H2SO4 input also dissolves calcium phosphate in the substrate, which increases the phosphate solubility. The increase in the SO concentration in the liquid likely led to the increase in the H2S concentration over time (Fig. 2; Olivera et al., 2022). When the pH is increased through NaOH after the first cycle of the filter press, the P can precipitate as struvite in the filter cake produced from the second cycle of the filter press (Szogi

Fig. 5. The nutrients in the AD1 influent, AD2 effluent, and recycled water from the filter press to the front of the AD system, with the total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN) shown in (a), the total phosphorus (TP) shown in (b), and the total ammonium nitrogen (NH4-N) concentration (mg/L) shown in (c). The TKN and TP concentrations of the solid products from the solid separator and second cycle of the filter press from Months 3-22 are shown in (d).

The NH4 concentration increased 407 % in the AD1 influent from Month 1 (550 mg/L) to Month 22 (2793 mg/L) (p-value = 0.0001) due to the high NH4 concentration in the recycled water, which increased from 741 mg/L (Month 1) to 2921 mg (Month 22) (p-value <0.0001), with a high average NH4-N concentration in the AD1 effluent (2037 mg/ L) and AD2 effluent (1821 mg/L). There was no evidence of NH3 toxicity in the AD system, but the pH did increase in the recycling the water from 7.1 (Month 1) to 8.2 (Month 22) (Fig. 5).

The digestion process is sensitive to pH, with the biological processes having a pH range of 6.6-7.8 for optimal CH4 production (Eryildiz et al., 2020). The pH in the AD system increased from 7.6 to 8.2 after more than 2 years of recycling the water to the front of the system, with a pH 7.8 higher considered above the optimal pH range (6.6-7.8) for CH4 production (Fu et al., 2021). The increase in pH was likely related to the increase in NH4-N concentration in the recycled water (Fig. 4). In Month 20, the biogas production was at a peak when the pH dropped below 8.0 to 7.9, and then the biogas production decreased in Months 21 and 22 when the pH was >8.0 (Fig. 6).
25: The biogas production had average CH4 concentrations slightly higher (64 %) than the range reported in other PL digestion studies (58-62%) (Bayrakdar et al., 2018; Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019). The average normalized CH4 production was 112 L CH4/kg VS but rose to 222 L CH4/kg VS during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) of the study period (Fig. 2). The CH4 production during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) was significantly higher than in Months 1-3 and Months 17-19 (p-values = 0.0179 and 0.0318, respectively). Previous research on PL digestion has shown digestion efficiency values ranging from 150 to 300 L CH4/kg VS (Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019; Hassanein et al., 2019). The average CH4 production in this sys- tem (112 L CH4/kg VS) was below this range, likely due to non- acclimatized microbes during the AD startup. The average H2S concentration was 14,458 ppm before the H2S scrubber and 7597 ppm after the scrubber (Fig. 2) (p-value <0.0001), resulting in an average H2S removal efficacy of 47.6 with >85.0 % H2S removal efficiency during Months 1-4. The H2S scrubber efficiency decreased with the increase in the H2S concentration (>10,000 ppm) in the raw biogas over a 16-month period (Months 5-15) (Fig. 2), showing H2S scrubber failure, which was remedied after this present study concluded by switching out the media and increasing microaeration within the AD system.

The H2S content in the biogas produced from PL digestion can be as high as 30,000 ppm, which can inhibit CH4 production and requires efficient scrubbing equipment to purify the biogas before it use in an engine generator, boiler, or fuel cell systems (Yan et al., 2018). Scrub- bing of H2S from the biogas is recommended prior to using the biogas in a generator or boiler, as H2S is a highly corrosive and hazardous gas (Shelford et al., 2019). The H2S concentrations in the raw biogas from this study were all above the recommended maximum H2S concentra- tion for biogas end use in a boiler (1000 ppm), engine-generator set (500 ppm), vehicle fuel (23 ppm), pipeline injection (4 ppm), and fuel cell (1 ppm) (Shelford et al., 2019).
3.2. Total solids (TS) and volatile solids (VS) concentrations
When the percentage of the VS in an AD system increases, there can

be a corresponding increase in the produced biogas. However, if the biological capacity of organisms in the digester to process the VS is exceeded, the pH can decrease from excessive VFA formation, resulting in a reduction in biogas production. In this system, the AD1 influent TS significantly increased over time from 2.59 % (month 1) to 5.73 % (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001), with an average influent TS of 2.92% (Fig. 3). The average influent VS content was .6%, increasing from 0.9 (Month 1) to 3.59% VS (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001); Fig. 3). The TS and VS were significantly reduced from the AD1 influent to the AD2 effluent by 30.9 and 35.9 % (p-values <0.0001), respectively, as the organic matter was converted to CH4. With solids removed in the screw press separator and filter press, the total reduction in TS and VS from the AD1 influent to the recycled water effluent was 48.1 and 57.6 %, respectively (p-values <0.0001 and 0.0005, respectively) (Fig. 3).

During the final year of monitoring for this study (Months 11-22), the cumulative solids produced from eight runs of the nutrient capture system were 6.2 tons of separated solids (2.1 tons DW), 15.6 tons of solids from the first cycle of the filter press (6.0 tons DW), and 1.1 tons of solids from the second cycle of the filter press (0.5 tons DW). All the recycled water (averaging 147 m³ /month) was recovered from the filter press, stored, and then mixed with the PL and additional well water (averaging 0.41 m³ /month) to feed the AD system, with no liquid dis- charged from the AD system; only the three solid products left the sys- tem as a fertilizer source.
3.3. Total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN), total phosphorus (TP), and ammonium-nitrogen (NH4-N) concentrations

Unprocessed PL is often directly applied to cropland to take advan- tage of its high nutrient content. But in some poultry producing regions, such as the Chesapeake Bay region in the MidAtlantic US, over- application of manure has led to high soil phosphorus content, with runoff from those areas contributing to eutrophication of the Ches- apeake Bay (Liu et al., 2018). In the 22-month monitoring period, the average of TKN and TP of the unprocessed PL were 37,239 mg N/kg, and 15,305 mg P/kg, respectively (Fig. 4). The average concentrations of TKN and TP in the recycled water were 2931 mg N/L, and 192 mg P/L, respectively, resulting in the average AD1 influent concentration for the combined PL and recycled water to be 3014 mg N/L and 415 mg P/L, respectively, with the high nutrient value in the diluted substrate due to the nutrients still present in the recycled water (Fig. 4). The AD1 influent TKN concentrations in the mixing tank increased by 133 % from Month (1739 mg N/L) to Month 22 (4069 mg N/L) (p-value = 0.0402), as the VS loading rate to the digester increased and the use of recirculated water increased the nutrient content overtime in the influent material (Fig. 5). The AD1 TP concentrations increased in the mixing tank by 54.5% from Month 1 (231 mg P/L) to Month 22 (357 mg P/L) (p-value 0.172), but this increase was not significant as most of the P remained in the solids, which were removed from the system through solids sep- aration and the filter press before recycling the water to the front of the system (Figs. 4 and 5).

The solids separator and filter press were operated intermittently during the monitoring period. From Months 11 to 22, 122 tons of PL (84.6 tons DW, consisting of 3.7 tons TKN and 1.9 tons TP) were added to the AD and nutrient capture system mixed with 147 m³ recycled water/month and 0.41 m³ well water/month. The recycled water con- tained solids and nutrients, resulting in 2.9 tons DW solids/month in the recycled water containing 0.44 tons TKN/month and 0.03 tons TP/ month. The cumulative solids collected from the eight runs of the solid separator were 6220 kg (2126 kg DW, containing 41.2 kg TKN and 19.7 kg TP). The cumulative filter cake solids collected from the first run of the filter press were 15,630 kg (6220 kg DW, containing 83 kg TKN and 40 kg TP), with 1.1 tons of filter cake collected from the second cycle of the filter press over the eight runs (529 kg DW, containing 8.7 kg TKN, and 9.3 kg TP). The average TKN concentration in the separated solids was 19,394 mg N/kg DW, and the average TP concentration was 9270

After the solids separator and before the filter press, an acid tank was used to strip ammonia from the liquid phase to the solid phase, as an ammonium sulfate solid, to increase the amount of NH4 in the filter cake fertilizer and reduce the NH4 concentration remaining in the recycle water (Törnwall et al., 2017). During the final year of monitoring (Months 11-22), there was 273 kg of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) added to the

acid tank, and 82 kg of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) added to the liquid fraction after the first cycle of the filter press. Previous studies have shown that H2SO4 addition can remove between 40 and 80 % of NH4 from the liquid, depending on the quantity of H2SO4 added (Guo et al., 2009; Jurgutis et al., 2020; Törnwall et al., 2017). A H2SO4 input also dissolves calcium phosphate in the substrate, which increases the phosphate solubility. The increase in the SO concentration in the liquid likely led to the increase in the H2S concentration over time (Fig. 2; Olivera et al., 2022). When the pH is increased through NaOH after the first cycle of the filter press, the P can precipitate as struvite in the filter cake produced from the second cycle of the filter press (Szogi

Fig. 5. The nutrients in the AD1 influent, AD2 effluent, and recycled water from the filter press to the front of the AD system, with the total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN) shown in (a), the total phosphorus (TP) shown in (b), and the total ammonium nitrogen (NH4-N) concentration (mg/L) shown in (c). The TKN and TP concentrations of the solid products from the solid separator and second cycle of the filter press from Months 3-22 are shown in (d).

The NH4 concentration increased 407 % in the AD1 influent from Month 1 (550 mg/L) to Month 22 (2793 mg/L) (p-value = 0.0001) due to the high NH4 concentration in the recycled water, which increased from 741 mg/L (Month 1) to 2921 mg (Month 22) (p-value <0.0001), with a high average NH4-N concentration in the AD1 effluent (2037 mg/ L) and AD2 effluent (1821 mg/L). There was no evidence of NH3 toxicity in the AD system, but the pH did increase in the recycling the water from 7.1 (Month 1) to 8.2 (Month 22) (Fig. 5).

The digestion process is sensitive to pH, with the biological processes having a pH range of 6.6-7.8 for optimal CH4 production (Eryildiz et al., 2020). The pH in the AD system increased from 7.6 to 8.2 after more than 2 years of recycling the water to the front of the system, with a pH 7.8 higher considered above the optimal pH range (6.6-7.8) for CH4 production (Fu et al., 2021). The increase in pH was likely related to the increase in NH4-N concentration in the recycled water (Fig. 4). In Month 20, the biogas production was at a peak when the pH dropped below 8.0 to 7.9, and then the biogas production decreased in Months 21 and 22 when the pH was >8.0 (Fig. 6).
27: The biogas production had average CH4 concentrations slightly higher (64 %) than the range reported in other PL digestion studies (58-62%) (Bayrakdar et al., 2018; Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019). The average normalized CH4 production was 112 L CH4/kg VS but rose to 222 L CH4/kg VS during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) of the study period (Fig. 2). The CH4 production during the last 3 months (Months 20-22) was significantly higher than in Months 1-3 and Months 17-19 (p-values = 0.0179 and 0.0318, respectively). Previous research on PL digestion has shown digestion efficiency values ranging from 150 to 300 L CH4/kg VS (Borowski et al., 2014; Chaump et al., 2019; Hassanein et al., 2019). The average CH4 production in this sys- tem (112 L CH4/kg VS) was below this range, likely due to non- acclimatized microbes during the AD startup. The average H2S concentration was 14,458 ppm before the H2S scrubber and 7597 ppm after the scrubber (Fig. 2) (p-value <0.0001), resulting in an average H2S removal efficacy of 47.6 with >85.0 % H2S removal efficiency during Months 1-4. The H2S scrubber efficiency decreased with the increase in the H2S concentration (>10,000 ppm) in the raw biogas over a 16-month period (Months 5-15) (Fig. 2), showing H2S scrubber failure, which was remedied after this present study concluded by switching out the media and increasing microaeration within the AD system.

The H2S content in the biogas produced from PL digestion can be as high as 30,000 ppm, which can inhibit CH4 production and requires efficient scrubbing equipment to purify the biogas before it use in an engine generator, boiler, or fuel cell systems (Yan et al., 2018). Scrub- bing of H2S from the biogas is recommended prior to using the biogas in a generator or boiler, as H2S is a highly corrosive and hazardous gas (Shelford et al., 2019). The H2S concentrations in the raw biogas from this study were all above the recommended maximum H2S concentra- tion for biogas end use in a boiler (1000 ppm), engine-generator set (500 ppm), vehicle fuel (23 ppm), pipeline injection (4 ppm), and fuel cell (1 ppm) (Shelford et al., 2019).
3.2. Total solids (TS) and volatile solids (VS) concentrations
When the percentage of the VS in an AD system increases, there can

be a corresponding increase in the produced biogas. However, if the biological capacity of organisms in the digester to process the VS is exceeded, the pH can decrease from excessive VFA formation, resulting in a reduction in biogas production. In this system, the AD1 influent TS significantly increased over time from 2.59 % (month 1) to 5.73 % (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001), with an average influent TS of 2.92% (Fig. 3). The average influent VS content was .6%, increasing from 0.9 (Month 1) to 3.59% VS (Month 21) (p-value <0.0001); Fig. 3). The TS and VS were significantly reduced from the AD1 influent to the AD2 effluent by 30.9 and 35.9 % (p-values <0.0001), respectively, as the organic matter was converted to CH4. With solids removed in the screw press separator and filter press, the total reduction in TS and VS from the AD1 influent to the recycled water effluent was 48.1 and 57.6 %, respectively (p-values <0.0001 and 0.0005, respectively) (Fig. 3).

During the final year of monitoring for this study (Months 11-22), the cumulative solids produced from eight runs of the nutrient capture system were 6.2 tons of separated solids (2.1 tons DW), 15.6 tons of solids from the first cycle of the filter press (6.0 tons DW), and 1.1 tons of solids from the second cycle of the filter press (0.5 tons DW). All the recycled water (averaging 147 m³ /month) was recovered from the filter press, stored, and then mixed with the PL and additional well water (averaging 0.41 m³ /month) to feed the AD system, with no liquid dis- charged from the AD system; only the three solid products left the sys- tem as a fertilizer source.
3.3. Total Kjeldahl nitrogen (TKN), total phosphorus (TP), and ammonium-nitrogen (NH4-N) concentrations

Unprocessed PL is often directly applied to cropland to take advan- tage of its high nutrient content. But in some poultry producing regions, such as the Chesapeake Bay region in the MidAtlantic US, over- application of manure has led to high soil phosphorus content, with runoff from those areas contributing to eutrophication of the Ches- apeake Bay (Liu et al., 2018). In the 22-month monitoring period, the average of TKN and TP of the unprocessed PL were 37,239 mg N/kg, and 15,305 mg P/kg, respectively (Fig. 4). The average concentrations of TKN and TP in the recycled water were 2931 mg N/L, and 192 mg P/L, respectively, resulting in the average AD1 influent concentration for the combined PL and recycled water to be 3014 mg N/L and 415 mg P/L, respectively, with the high nutrient value in the diluted substrate due to the nutrients still present in the recycled water (Fig. 4). The AD1 influent TKN concentrations in the mixing tank increased by 133 % from Month (1739 mg N/L) to Month 22 (4069 mg N/L) (p-value = 0.0402), as the VS loading rate to the digester increased and the use of recirculated water increased the nutrient content overtime in the influent material (Fig. 5). The AD1 TP concentrations increased in the mixing tank by 54.5% from Month 1 (231 mg P/L) to Month 22 (357 mg P/L) (p-value 0.172), but this increase was not significant as most of the P remained in the solids, which were removed from the system through solids sep- aration and the filter press before recycling the water to the front of the system (Figs. 4 and 5).

The solids separator and filter press were operated intermittently during the monitoring period. From Months 11 to 22, 122 tons of PL (84.6 tons DW, consisting of 3.7 tons TKN and 1.9 tons TP) were added to the AD and nutrient capture system mixed with 147 m³ recycled water/month and 0.41 m³ well water/month. The recycled water con- tained solids and nutrients, resulting in 2.9 tons DW solids/month in the recycled water containing 0.44 tons TKN/month and 0.03 tons TP/ month. The cumulative solids collected from the eight runs of the solid separator were 6220 kg (2126 kg DW, containing 41.2 kg TKN and 19.7 kg TP). The cumulative filter cake solids collected from the first run of the filter press were 15,630 kg (6220 kg DW, containing 83 kg TKN and 40 kg TP), with 1.1 tons of filter cake collected from the second cycle of the filter press over the eight runs (529 kg DW, containing 8.7 kg TKN, and 9.3 kg TP). The average TKN concentration in the separated solids was 19,394 mg N/kg DW, and the average TP concentration was 9270

After the solids separator and before the filter press, an acid tank was used to strip ammonia from the liquid phase to the solid phase, as an ammonium sulfate solid, to increase the amount of NH4 in the filter cake fertilizer and reduce the NH4 concentration remaining in the recycle water (Törnwall et al., 2017). During the final year of monitoring (Months 11-22), there was 273 kg of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) added to the

acid tank, and 82 kg of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) added to the liquid fraction after the first cycle of the filter press. Previous studies have shown that H2SO4 addition can remove between 40 and 80 % of NH4 from the liquid, depending on the quantity of H2SO4 added (Guo et al., 2009; Jurgutis et al., 2020; Törnwall et al., 2017). A H2SO4 input also dissolves calcium phosphate in the substrate, which increases the phosphate solubility. The increase in the SO concentration in the liquid likely led to the increase in the H2S concentration over time (Fig. 2; Olivera et al., 2022). When the pH is increased through NaOH after the first cycle of the filter press, the P can precipitate as struvite in the filter cake produced from the second cycle of the filter press (Szogi
