By replacing propane with biogas (Scenario B), impacts were reduced compared to the Scenario using the highest CH4 production for global warming (-86.2%), ozone depletion (-103%), smog (-50.8 %), acidification (-38.9 ° eutrophication (-37.2%), carcinogens (-9.8 non-carcinogens (-12.6 respi- ratory effects (-41.5%), ecotoxicity (-10.9%), and fossil fuel depletion (-111%). Furthermore, replacing the boiler or generator use with 100 % CHP use (Scenario C) at the same PL loading rate reduced impacts compared to the project's 'Highest CH4 Production' analysis for global warming (-85.5 9 ozone depletion (-94.6%) and fossil fuel deple- tion (-95.6%).

These LCA results were similar to previous research showing that using a CHP had greater environmental savings compared with biogas used in other applications (Beausang et al., 2020). Another study found that using a CHP reduces the environmental impact compared to upgrading the biogas to renewable natural gas (Tsapekos et al., 2019). When doubling and tripling the PL substrate and using a CHP (Scenarios D and E), the systems had 143 % and 161 %, respectively, lower global warming impacts of compared to 'Highest CH4 Production' analysis when the fertilizer product was not transported off-site (0 km).

The LCA results for transporting the solid fertilizer product 40 km using the 'Project Current Results' data showed higher Climate Change than the Baseline scenario due to the high amount of propane used for heating the digester. If 100 % of the biogas had been used in the boiler (Scenario B) or in a CHP generator (Scenario C), the Climate Change impact would still be 45.6 % and 54.0 % higher than the Baseline Sce- nario, respectively. If double (Scenario D) or triple (Scenario E) the amount of PL substrate was processed in the system with a CHP, the Climate Change impact would be 528 % and 722 % less than the Base- line scenario, respectively. Additionally, the results showed large re- ductions in fossil fuel depletion in Scenario B (-320 %), Scenario D (-1419 and Scenario E (-1897 %) compared to the Baseline Sce- nario (Fig. 7). The 80 km transportation scenario showed a similar trend to 40 km scenarios, with large reductions in impact when the biogas was used in the boiler (i.e., no propane use; Scenario B) or used in a CHP
generator with double and triple the PL loading rate (Fig. 7).

Overall, the results showed that AD and nutrient capture using the conditions in Scenarios B, D, and E led to net environmental gains, as the system would produce enough biogas to eliminate propane use for AD heating and cover the electricity needed to run the pumps, separators, and filter press. Scenarios B, D, and E also showed reductions in ozone depletion, global warming, smog, acidification, eutrophication, carcin- ogens, non-carcinogens, respiratory effects, ecotoxicity, and fossil fuel depletion compared to transporting unprocessed PL 40 or 80 km from the farm. Specifically, in the eutrophication potential category, tripling the amount of PL processed in the system with 100 % of the biogas used in a CHP generator had the lowest impacts, with 510% less impacts than the Baseline (transporting unprocessed PL 40 km). The 'Project Current' eutrophication potential was 1336 % higher than the Baseline. The re- sults showed that eliminating the use of propane, increasing the PL loading rate in the system, and using the biogas for AD heating (boiler use) or in a CHP would result in lower environmental impacts (i.e., more sustainable) than the Baseline scenario of transporting unprocessed PL 40 or 80 km roundtrip. Operating the AD and nutrient capture system under more optimal conditions (Scenarios B, D, and E) could present a more sustainable alternative to PL transportation in the Chesapeake Bay region (US), or any location with PL land application restrictions. These results are in agreement with an LCA study of PL, which found AD was a suitable disposal route for PL (Beausang et al., 2020).
44: The biological processes responsible for CH4 production are affected by the AD temperature and temperature fluctuations. AD systems are generally operated in the mesophilic (30-37 °C) or thermophilic (50-60 °C) temperature range. The results showed the average AD temperature was 33.6 + 0.1 °C, which was within the mesophilic tem- perature range (30-37 °C), but the AD temperature dropped below the mesophilic range during low ambient temperatures (Fig. 5). Two heat- ing sources (propane and biogas) were used to maintain the AD
Fig. 6. The study results for pH shown in (a) and temperature shown in (b).

temperature, with propane used when the H2S scrubber was not effec- tive in bringing the H2S level in the biogas below 1000 ppm for boiler use to heat the AD system. The lower temperature likely resulted in lower overall AD efficiency (112 L CH4/kg VS) during the 22-month monitoring period.
3.5. Volatile fatty acids (VFAs), chemical oxygen demand (COD) and soluble (sCOD) concentrations

The average VFAs (acetic, propionic, and butyric) concentrations were higher in the AD1 influent (31.2, 6.2, and 2.1 nM, respectively) compared to the AD2 effluent (6.4, 1.5, and 0.4 mM, respectively) (p- values 0.0062, 0.0066, and 0.0017, respectively), as the VFAs were converted into CH4 during AD. The average valeric acid concentration was not significantly different in the AD1 influent mM) and AD2 effluent 1.2mM) (p-value 0.3323). The VFA concentrations in the last 3 months of monitoring (Months 20-22) were high due to a corre- sponding increase in the substrate loading rate. Wang et al. (2009) showed that a high concentration of propionic acid and valeric acid can significantly affect the activity of methanogens. In this study, the acetic acid concentration was higher than propionic acid, butyric acid, and valeric acid, showing no instability due to VFAs with effluent VFA concentrations much lower than influent VFA concentrations.

The COD concentration indicates the amount of organic matter available for the microorganisms in the AD system, and thus, indicative of the CH4 potential of the substrate entering the AD system. The average COD concentration in the AD1 influent between Month 1 and Month 6 was 25.2 g/L, which was a significant reduction from the AD2 effluent COD concentration of 9.4 g/L (p-value <0.0001), indicating that 62.7 of the COD was transformed to biogas during digestion. The average AD1 influent sCOD from Months 7 to 22 was 13.8 g/L, and the AD2 effluent averaged 10.6 g/L, with a 23.2 % sCOD removal (p-value <0.0001). The COD and sCOD reduction from the influent of AD1 to the liquid effluent from the filter press were 67.8 and 50.3 respectively (p-values <0.0001). High COD removal indicates a high organic matter conversion rate to biogas and/or high solids removal from solid sepa- ration and the filter press.
3.6. Life cycle assessment (LCA) and energy balance

During the year of monitoring used for the LCA (Months 11-22), the AD system processed 122.3 tons/year of PL, with 10,106 m³ biogas produced/yr (2.3*108 BTU/yr), with 6.7% of the biogas (504 m³ used to produce electricity (460 kW with a 15 % conversion efficiency; 51,713 kWh/yr), 22.1 % of the biogas (2232 /yr) used in the boiler for digester heating (12.1 of the heat energy required), and 71.1% of the biogas (7189 m³/yr) flared. During Months 20-22, the system operated with a higher efficiency, processing 23.9 tons of PL from Months 20-22, producing 4471 m³ biogas (2937 m³ CH4) during this time. The scrubbing process was not operating properly for most of the study, as H2S concentrations >10,000 ppm (Fig. 2) prevented the use of the biogas in the generator or boiler.

The energy balance depends on the following: 1) the AD energy production, i.e., volume of biogas production and the CH4 concentration in the biogas, which are affected by the AD process parameters, such as loading rate, AD temperature, and pH, 2) the electricity needed for pumping and mixing in the AD system and to run the filter press and solid separator, and 3) propane used for AD heating when the biogas cannot be used in the boiler due to high H2S concentrations. The biogas produced over the last year (Months 11-22) was equivalent to 2.3.108 BTUs/yr, with 16.2 m³ of propane (3.92.108 BTU/yr) combusted for AD heating, and 1.76.108 BTUs/yr of electricity used, resulting in a net energy consumption of 3.4.108 BTUs/yr. The net energy balance was negative (more energy was used than created) due to the large amount of propane used to heat the large liquid volume in the two ADs. To achieve a positive energy balance, the system could increase the PL loading rate

to increase the biogas production, flare less biogas, use the biogas for heating, or use the biogas in a CHP generator to heat the AD system and produce electricity. During the monitoring period, most of the biogas was flared (71.1%) compared to boiler use (22.1 %) and generator use (6.8 %), and there- fore, the electricity and heat energy produced from the digester was only a fraction of the total energy used to run the system. With most of the energy flared (i.e., not utilized), the utilized energy from biogas pro- duction was not sufficient to offset the parasitic energy required for daily system operation. The Baseline Scenario investigated transporting the unprocessed PL 40 to 80 km from the farm site (Table 1). Scenarios A-E evaluated using 100% of biogas used to produce electricity and/or heat energy, with none of the biogas flared. Double the substrate input (244 tons PL/year) was used in Scenario D and triple the substrate input (366

tons PL/year) was used in Scenario E, with the biogas used in CHP system in both scenarios. Each scenario looked at using the fertilizer product in the immediate surrounding farms (0 km) or trucking the solid fertilizer product 40 or 80 km to allow comparisons with the Baseline.

The LCA showed that the greenhouse gas emissions (global warming category) were 20.3 % lower when using the highest CH4 production (Months 20-22) was used compared to the yearly average CH4 pro- duction (Months 11-22) (Fig. 7). Additionally, the LCA showed de- creases in the following categories when using the highest CH4 production data compared to the yearly average CH4 production: ozone depletion (-18.9 %), smog (-13.3 %), acidification (-11.4 %), eutro- phication (-14.5%) carcinogens (-4.8%), non-carcinogens (-4.3%), respiratory effects (-15.2%), ecotoxicity (-8.3 %), and fossil fuel depletion (-13.7% Fig. 7). In this study, the acetic acid concentration was higher than propionic acid, butyric acid, and valeric acid, showing no instability due to VFAs with effluent VFA concentrations much lower than influent VFA concentrations.

The COD concentration indicates the amount of organic matter available for the microorganisms in the AD system, and thus, indicative of the CH4 potential of the substrate entering the AD system. The average COD concentration in the AD1 influent between Month 1 and Month 6 was 25.2 g/L, which was a significant reduction from the AD2 effluent COD concentration of 9.4 g/L (p-value <0.0001), indicating that 62.7 of the COD was transformed to biogas during digestion. The average AD1 influent sCOD from Months 7 to 22 was 13.8 g/L, and the AD2 effluent averaged 10.6 g/L, with a 23.2 % sCOD removal (p-value <0.0001). The COD and sCOD reduction from the influent of AD1 to the liquid effluent from the filter press were 67.8 and 50.3 respectively (p-values <0.0001). High COD removal indicates a high organic matter conversion rate to biogas and/or high solids removal from solid sepa- ration and the filter press.
3.6. Life cycle assessment (LCA) and energy balance

During the year of monitoring used for the LCA (Months 11-22), the AD system processed 122.3 tons/year of PL, with 10,106 m³ biogas produced/yr (2.3*108 BTU/yr), with 6.7% of the biogas (504 m³ used to produce electricity (460 kW with a 15 % conversion efficiency; 51,713 kWh/yr), 22.1 % of the biogas (2232 /yr) used in the boiler for digester heating (12.1 of the heat energy required), and 71.1% of the biogas (7189 m³/yr) flared. During Months 20-22, the system operated with a higher efficiency, processing 23.9 tons of PL from Months 20-22, producing 4471 m³ biogas (2937 m³ CH4) during this time. The scrubbing process was not operating properly for most of the study, as H2S concentrations >10,000 ppm (Fig. 2) prevented the use of the biogas in the generator or boiler.

The energy balance depends on the following: 1) the AD energy production, i.e., volume of biogas production and the CH4 concentration in the biogas, which are affected by the AD process parameters, such as loading rate, AD temperature, and pH, 2) the electricity needed for pumping and mixing in the AD system and to run the filter press and solid separator, and 3) propane used for AD heating when the biogas cannot be used in the boiler due to high H2S concentrations. The biogas produced over the last year (Months 11-22) was equivalent to 2.3.108 BTUs/yr, with 16.2 m³ of propane (3.92.108 BTU/yr) combusted for AD heating, and 1.76.108 BTUs/yr of electricity used, resulting in a net energy consumption of 3.4.108 BTUs/yr. The net energy balance was negative (more energy was used than created) due to the large amount of propane used to heat the large liquid volume in the two ADs. To achieve a positive energy balance, the system could increase the PL loading rate

to increase the biogas production, flare less biogas, use the biogas for heating, or use the biogas in a CHP generator to heat the AD system and produce electricity. During the monitoring period, most of the biogas was flared (71.1%) compared to boiler use (22.1 %) and generator use (6.8 %), and there- fore, the electricity and heat energy produced from the digester was only a fraction of the total energy used to run the system. With most of the energy flared (i.e., not utilized), the utilized energy from biogas pro- duction was not sufficient to offset the parasitic energy required for daily system operation. The Baseline Scenario investigated transporting the unprocessed PL 40 to 80 km from the farm site (Table 1). Scenarios A-E evaluated using 100% of biogas used to produce electricity and/or heat energy, with none of the biogas flared. Double the substrate input (244 tons PL/year) was used in Scenario D and triple the substrate input (366

tons PL/year) was used in Scenario E, with the biogas used in CHP system in both scenarios. Each scenario looked at using the fertilizer product in the immediate surrounding farms (0 km) or trucking the solid fertilizer product 40 or 80 km to allow comparisons with the Baseline.

The LCA showed that the greenhouse gas emissions (global warming category) were 20.3 % lower when using the highest CH4 production (Months 20-22) was used compared to the yearly average CH4 pro- duction (Months 11-22) (Fig. 7). Additionally, the LCA showed de- creases in the following categories when using the highest CH4 production data compared to the yearly average CH4 production: ozone depletion (-18.9 %), smog (-13.3 %), acidification (-11.4 %), eutro- phication (-14.5%) carcinogens (-4.8%), non-carcinogens (-4.3%), respiratory effects (-15.2%), ecotoxicity (-8.3 %), and fossil fuel depletion (-13.7% Fig. 7). The yearly average data (Months 11-22)

Fig. 7. The life cycle assessment (LCA) results showing the environmental impacts of the different scenarios compared to Baseline scenario, with transportation of the unprocessed poultry litter (PL) or fertilizer solids 40 km shown in (a), transportation of unprocessed PL or fertilizer solid product 80 km shown in (b).

and the highest CH4 production data (Months 20-22) without the fer- tilizer solids transported off-site (0 km) had higher environmental im- pacts than the Baseline Scenario (unprocessed PL transported 40 km) in the Climate Change category (1170 and 916 %, respectively) due to propane used for digester heating. Scenario B (biogas used in a boiler) and Scenario C (biogas used in a CHP) also had higher environmental impacts compared to the Baseline Scenario (unprocessed PL transported 40 km) in the Climate Change category (39.6 and 47.9 %, respec- tively). If double (Scenario D) or triple (Scenario E) the quantity of PL substrate was processed in the AD system with a CHP (0 km), then the environmental impacts were less (-534 % and - 729 %, respectively) than the Baseline Scenario, with a significant reduction in Scenario D compared to the Baseline Scenario (p-value = 0.0388) and a significant reduction in impacts with Scenario E compared to the Baseline Scenario (p-value = 0.0072). There were large reductions in fossil fuel depletion compared to the Baseline Scenario (40 km) in Scenario B (-339%) Scenario C (-2.3% Scenario D (-1438%) and Scenario E (-1917%) (Fig. 7). These results highlight the importance of maintaining high biogas production rates to increase the overall system sustainability and not using propane for AD heating to reduce the environmental impacts associated with AD system processing and manufacturing.

Scenarios B-E were more sustainable than Scenario A (100 % biogas use in the generator) when the fertilizer product was not transported off- site (0 km) (p-values < 0.001, < 0.001, 0.001, 0.0052, and 0.0091, respectively) (Fig. 7). In Scenario A, propane use was still needed to heat the AD system, as the generator was not a CHP generator. Propane use increased the impacts of the system compared to using the biogas in the boiler for AD heating or using the biogas in a CHP generator. In Scenario B, all the biogas was used in boiler, which offset all the energy required for AD heating, eliminating the need for propane. The 'Current Project Results' analysis showed that propane consumption was the main source of the negative impacts on the system. By replacing propane with biogas (Scenario B), impacts were reduced compared to the Scenario using the highest CH4 production for global warming (-86.2%), ozone depletion (-103%), smog (-50.8 %), acidification (-38.9 ° eutrophication (-37.2%), carcinogens (-9.8 non-carcinogens (-12.6 respi- ratory effects (-41.5%), ecotoxicity (-10.9%), and fossil fuel depletion (-111%). Furthermore, replacing the boiler or generator use with 100 % CHP use (Scenario C) at the same PL loading rate reduced impacts compared to the project's 'Highest CH4 Production' analysis for global warming (-85.5 9 ozone depletion (-94.6%) and fossil fuel deple- tion (-95.6%).

These LCA results were similar to previous research showing that using a CHP had greater environmental savings compared with biogas used in other applications (Beausang et al., 2020). Another study found that using a CHP reduces the environmental impact compared to upgrading the biogas to renewable natural gas (Tsapekos et al., 2019). When doubling and tripling the PL substrate and using a CHP (Scenarios D and E), the systems had 143 % and 161 %, respectively, lower global warming impacts of compared to 'Highest CH4 Production' analysis when the fertilizer product was not transported off-site (0 km).

The LCA results for transporting the solid fertilizer product 40 km using the 'Project Current Results' data showed higher Climate Change than the Baseline scenario due to the high amount of propane used for heating the digester. If 100 % of the biogas had been used in the boiler (Scenario B) or in a CHP generator (Scenario C), the Climate Change impact would still be 45.6 % and 54.0 % higher than the Baseline Sce- nario, respectively. If double (Scenario D) or triple (Scenario E) the amount of PL substrate was processed in the system with a CHP, the Climate Change impact would be 528 % and 722 % less than the Base- line scenario, respectively. Additionally, the results showed large re- ductions in fossil fuel depletion in Scenario B (-320 %), Scenario D (-1419 and Scenario E (-1897 %) compared to the Baseline Sce- nario (Fig. 7). The 80 km transportation scenario showed a similar trend to 40 km scenarios, with large reductions in impact when the biogas was used in the boiler (i.e., no propane use; Scenario B) or used in a CHP
generator with double and triple the PL loading rate (Fig. 7).

Overall, the results showed that AD and nutrient capture using the conditions in Scenarios B, D, and E led to net environmental gains, as the system would produce enough biogas to eliminate propane use for AD heating and cover the electricity needed to run the pumps, separators, and filter press. Scenarios B, D, and E also showed reductions in ozone depletion, global warming, smog, acidification, eutrophication, carcin- ogens, non-carcinogens, respiratory effects, ecotoxicity, and fossil fuel depletion compared to transporting unprocessed PL 40 or 80 km from the farm. Specifically, in the eutrophication potential category, tripling the amount of PL processed in the system with 100 % of the biogas used in a CHP generator had the lowest impacts, with 510% less impacts than the Baseline (transporting unprocessed PL 40 km). The 'Project Current' eutrophication potential was 1336 % higher than the Baseline. The re- sults showed that eliminating the use of propane, increasing the PL loading rate in the system, and using the biogas for AD heating (boiler use) or in a CHP would result in lower environmental impacts (i.e., more sustainable) than the Baseline scenario of transporting unprocessed PL 40 or 80 km roundtrip. Operating the AD and nutrient capture system under more optimal conditions (Scenarios B, D, and E) could present a more sustainable alternative to PL transportation in the Chesapeake Bay region (US), or any location with PL land application restrictions. These results are in agreement with an LCA study of PL, which found AD was a suitable disposal route for PL (Beausang et al., 2020).

and the highest CH4 production data (Months 20-22) without the fer- tilizer solids transported off-site (0 km) had higher environmental im- pacts than the Baseline Scenario (unprocessed PL transported 40 km) in the Climate Change category (1170 and 916 %, respectively) due to propane used for digester heating. Scenario B (biogas used in a boiler) and Scenario C (biogas used in a CHP) also had higher environmental impacts compared to the Baseline Scenario (unprocessed PL transported 40 km) in the Climate Change category (39.6 and 47.9 %, respec- tively). If double (Scenario D) or triple (Scenario E) the quantity of PL substrate was processed in the AD system with a CHP (0 km), then the environmental impacts were less (-534 % and - 729 %, respectively) than the Baseline Scenario, with a significant reduction in Scenario D compared to the Baseline Scenario (p-value = 0.0388) and a significant reduction in impacts with Scenario E compared to the Baseline Scenario (p-value = 0.0072). There were large reductions in fossil fuel depletion compared to the Baseline Scenario (40 km) in Scenario B (-339%) Scenario C (-2.3% Scenario D (-1438%) and Scenario E (-1917%) (Fig. 7). These results highlight the importance of maintaining high biogas production rates to increase the overall system sustainability and not using propane for AD heating to reduce the environmental impacts associated with AD system processing and manufacturing.

Scenarios B-E were more sustainable than Scenario A (100 % biogas use in the generator) when the fertilizer product was not transported off- site (0 km) (p-values < 0.001, < 0.001, 0.001, 0.0052, and 0.0091, respectively) (Fig. 7). In Scenario A, propane use was still needed to heat the AD system, as the generator was not a CHP generator. Propane use increased the impacts of the system compared to using the biogas in the boiler for AD heating or using the biogas in a CHP generator. In Scenario B, all the biogas was used in boiler, which offset all the energy required for AD heating, eliminating the need for propane. The 'Current Project Results' analysis showed that propane consumption was the main source of the negative impacts on the system. By replacing propane with biogas (Scenario B), impacts were reduced compared to the Scenario using the highest CH4 production for global warming (-86.2%), ozone depletion (-103%), smog (-50.8 %), acidification (-38.9 ° eutrophication (-37.2%), carcinogens (-9.8 non-carcinogens (-12.6 respi- ratory effects (-41.5%), ecotoxicity (-10.9%), and fossil fuel depletion (-111%). Furthermore, replacing the boiler or generator use with 100 % CHP use (Scenario C) at the same PL loading rate reduced impacts compared to the project's 'Highest CH4 Production' analysis for global warming (-85.5 9 ozone depletion (-94.6%) and fossil fuel deple- tion (-95.6%).

These LCA results were similar to previous research showing that using a CHP had greater environmental savings compared with biogas used in other applications (Beausang et al., 2020). Another study found that using a CHP reduces the environmental impact compared to upgrading the biogas to renewable natural gas (Tsapekos et al., 2019). When doubling and tripling the PL substrate and using a CHP (Scenarios D and E), the systems had 143 % and 161 %, respectively, lower global warming impacts of compared to 'Highest CH4 Production' analysis when the fertilizer product was not transported off-site (0 km).

The LCA results for transporting the solid fertilizer product 40 km using the 'Project Current Results' data showed higher Climate Change than the Baseline scenario due to the high amount of propane used for heating the digester. If 100 % of the biogas had been used in the boiler (Scenario B) or in a CHP generator (Scenario C), the Climate Change impact would still be 45.6 % and 54.0 % higher than the Baseline Sce- nario, respectively. If double (Scenario D) or triple (Scenario E) the amount of PL substrate was processed in the system with a CHP, the Climate Change impact would be 528 % and 722 % less than the Base- line scenario, respectively. Additionally, the results showed large re- ductions in fossil fuel depletion in Scenario B (-320 %), Scenario D (-1419 and Scenario E (-1897 %) compared to the Baseline Sce- nario (Fig. 7). The 80 km transportation scenario showed a similar trend to 40 km scenarios, with large reductions in impact when the biogas was used in the boiler (i.e., no propane use; Scenario B) or used in a CHP
generator with double and triple the PL loading rate (Fig. 7).

Overall, the results showed that AD and nutrient capture using the conditions in Scenarios B, D, and E led to net environmental gains, as the system would produce enough biogas to eliminate propane use for AD heating and cover the electricity needed to run the pumps, separators, and filter press. Scenarios B, D, and E also showed reductions in ozone depletion, global warming, smog, acidification, eutrophication, carcin- ogens, non-carcinogens, respiratory effects, ecotoxicity, and fossil fuel depletion compared to transporting unprocessed PL 40 or 80 km from the farm. Specifically, in the eutrophication potential category, tripling the amount of PL processed in the system with 100 % of the biogas used in a CHP generator had the lowest impacts, with 510% less impacts than the Baseline (transporting unprocessed PL 40 km). The 'Project Current' eutrophication potential was 1336 % higher than the Baseline. The re- sults showed that eliminating the use of propane, increasing the PL loading rate in the system, and using the biogas for AD heating (boiler use) or in a CHP would result in lower environmental impacts (i.e., more sustainable) than the Baseline scenario of transporting unprocessed PL 40 or 80 km roundtrip. Operating the AD and nutrient capture system under more optimal conditions (Scenarios B, D, and E) could present a more sustainable alternative to PL transportation in the Chesapeake Bay region (US), or any location with PL land application restrictions. These results are in agreement with an LCA study of PL, which found AD was a suitable disposal route for PL (Beausang et al., 2020).

AD systems are generally operated in the mesophilic (30-37 °C) or thermophilic (50-60 °C) temperature range. The results showed the average AD temperature was 33.6 + 0.1 °C, which was within the mesophilic tem- perature range (30-37 °C), but the AD temperature dropped below the mesophilic range during low ambient temperatures (Fig. 5). Two heat- ing sources (propane and biogas) were used to maintain the AD
Fig. 6. The study results for pH shown in (a) and temperature shown in (b).

temperature, with propane used when the H2S scrubber was not effec- tive in bringing the H2S level in the biogas below 1000 ppm for boiler use to heat the AD system. The lower temperature likely resulted in lower overall AD efficiency (112 L CH4/kg VS) during the 22-month monitoring period.
3.5. Volatile fatty acids (VFAs), chemical oxygen demand (COD) and soluble (sCOD) concentrations

The average VFAs (acetic, propionic, and butyric) concentrations were higher in the AD1 influent (31.2, 6.2, and 2.1 nM, respectively) compared to the AD2 effluent (6.4, 1.5, and 0.4 mM, respectively) (p- values 0.0062, 0.0066, and 0.0017, respectively), as the VFAs were converted into CH4 during AD. The average valeric acid concentration was not significantly different in the AD1 influent mM) and AD2 effluent 1.2mM) (p-value 0.3323). The VFA concentrations in the last 3 months of monitoring (Months 20-22) were high due to a corre- sponding increase in the substrate loading rate. Wang et al. (2009) showed that a high concentration of propionic acid and valeric acid can significantly affect the activity of methanogens. In this study, the acetic acid concentration was higher than propionic acid, butyric acid, and valeric acid, showing no instability due to VFAs with effluent VFA concentrations much lower than influent VFA concentrations.

The COD concentration indicates the amount of organic matter available for the microorganisms in the AD system, and thus, indicative of the CH4 potential of the substrate entering the AD system. The average COD concentration in the AD1 influent between Month 1 and Month 6 was 25.2 g/L, which was a significant reduction from the AD2 effluent COD concentration of 9.4 g/L (p-value <0.0001), indicating that 62.7 of the COD was transformed to biogas during digestion. The average AD1 influent sCOD from Months 7 to 22 was 13.8 g/L, and the AD2 effluent averaged 10.6 g/L, with a 23.2 % sCOD removal (p-value <0.0001). The COD and sCOD reduction from the influent of AD1 to the liquid effluent from the filter press were 67.8 and 50.3 respectively (p-values <0.0001). High COD removal indicates a high organic matter conversion rate to biogas and/or high solids removal from solid sepa- ration and the filter press.
3.6. Life cycle assessment (LCA) and energy balance

During the year of monitoring used for the LCA (Months 11-22), the AD system processed 122.3 tons/year of PL, with 10,106 m³ biogas produced/yr (2.3*108 BTU/yr), with 6.7% of the biogas (504 m³ used to produce electricity (460 kW with a 15 % conversion efficiency; 51,713 kWh/yr), 22.1 % of the biogas (2232 /yr) used in the boiler for digester heating (12.1 of the heat energy required), and 71.1% of the biogas (7189 m³/yr) flared. During Months 20-22, the system operated with a higher efficiency, processing 23.9 tons of PL from Months 20-22, producing 4471 m³ biogas (2937 m³ CH4) during this time. The scrubbing process was not operating properly for most of the study, as H2S concentrations >10,000 ppm (Fig. 2) prevented the use of the biogas in the generator or boiler.

The energy balance depends on the following: 1) the AD energy production, i.e., volume of biogas production and the CH4 concentration in the biogas, which are affected by the AD process parameters, such as loading rate, AD temperature, and pH, 2) the electricity needed for pumping and mixing in the AD system and to run the filter press and solid separator, and 3) propane used for AD heating when the biogas cannot be used in the boiler due to high H2S concentrations. The biogas produced over the last year (Months 11-22) was equivalent to 2.3.108 BTUs/yr, with 16.2 m³ of propane (3.92.108 BTU/yr) combusted for AD heating, and 1.76.108 BTUs/yr of electricity used, resulting in a net energy consumption of 3.4.108 BTUs/yr. The net energy balance was negative (more energy was used than created) due to the large amount of propane used to heat the large liquid volume in the two ADs. To achieve a positive energy balance, the system could increase the PL loading rate

to increase the biogas production, flare less biogas, use the biogas for heating, or use the biogas in a CHP generator to heat the AD system and produce electricity. During the monitoring period, most of the biogas was flared (71.1%) compared to boiler use (22.1 %) and generator use (6.8 %), and there- fore, the electricity and heat energy produced from the digester was only a fraction of the total energy used to run the system. With most of the energy flared (i.e., not utilized), the utilized energy from biogas pro- duction was not sufficient to offset the parasitic energy required for daily system operation. The Baseline Scenario investigated transporting the unprocessed PL 40 to 80 km from the farm site (Table 1). Scenarios A-E evaluated using 100% of biogas used to produce electricity and/or heat energy, with none of the biogas flared. Double the substrate input (244 tons PL/year) was used in Scenario D and triple the substrate input (366

tons PL/year) was used in Scenario E, with the biogas used in CHP system in both scenarios. Each scenario looked at using the fertilizer product in the immediate surrounding farms (0 km) or trucking the solid fertilizer product 40 or 80 km to allow comparisons with the Baseline.

The LCA showed that the greenhouse gas emissions (global warming category) were 20.3 % lower when using the highest CH4 production (Months 20-22) was used compared to the yearly average CH4 pro- duction (Months 11-22) (Fig. 7). Additionally, the LCA showed de- creases in the following categories when using the highest CH4 production data compared to the yearly average CH4 production: ozone depletion (-18.9 %), smog (-13.3 %), acidification (-11.4 %), eutro- phication (-14.5%) carcinogens (-4.8%), non-carcinogens (-4.3%), respiratory effects (-15.2%), ecotoxicity (-8.3 %), and fossil fuel depletion (-13.7% Fig. 7). The 'Project Current' eutrophication potential was 1336 % higher than the Baseline. The re- sults showed that eliminating the use of propane, increasing the PL loading rate in the system, and using the biogas for AD heating (boiler use) or in a CHP would result in lower environmental impacts (i.e., more sustainable) than the Baseline scenario of transporting unprocessed PL 40 or 80 km roundtrip. Operating the AD and nutrient capture system under more optimal conditions (Scenarios B, D, and E) could present a more sustainable alternative to PL transportation in the Chesapeake Bay region (US), or any location with PL land application restrictions. These results are in agreement with an LCA study of PL, which found AD was a suitable disposal route for PL (Beausang et al., 2020).
51: The results showed that AD can be used to sustainably convert organic matter in PL to biogas, with the creation of a solids fertilizer and long-term water recycling in the AD system. The microbes in the AD adjusted to the rising ammonia rates, with the highest CH4 production rates (222 L CH4/kg VS) during the last 3 months of the 33-month AD operation. The system sustainability would be increased if the biogas was used in a CHP system without propane use for AD heating and use of a higher efficiency H2S scrubber.
